item.title: DataNexus Architecture: Four Layers and Two Flows
item.description: Why ontology-based routing instead of a single NL2SQL pipeline — and what the second flow, from Pull to Push, looks like

item.title: 4. SCD - When a Customer Moves, Where Were Past Orders Shipped?
item.description: When dimension data changes, do you overwrite history or preserve it? We implement SCD Type 1, 2, and 3 differences in SQL, then build a production pattern with dbt snapshot.

item.title: 3. Silver Layer - Promoting Bronze to an Analysis-Ready State
item.description: Cleanse and standardize the raw data stacked in Bronze. Fix types, unify column names, remove duplicates. We define this process as SQL models in dbt.

item.title: 2. Bronze Layer - Load the Source Data Exactly As-Is
item.description: There are two ways to load data into Bronze. Overwrite everything, or bring only what changed. Which one you choose completely changes the complexity of your pipeline.

item.title: 1. Medallion Architecture - Why We Stack Data in Three Layers
item.description: Bronze, Silver, Gold. What changes when you load data into separate layers. We build it hands-on with DuckDB and dbt.

item.title: 11. Designing Chat UIs for Uncertain AI Answers
item.description: How DataNexus shows answer evidence: SQL, Runtime Trace, fallback state, permission scope, sources, and the knowledge graph alongside the answer.

item.title: 10. Spider 72%: The Dataset, Not the Model, Shapes the Error Profile
item.description: Switching to Pro actually dropped accuracy by 8%p. I categorized 19 errors into 6 types and ran the same pipeline on Spider. The distributions were completely different. DB characteristics determine the error profile more than the model does.

item.title: 9. BIRD 56%: Nine Experiments and What Got Ruled Out
item.description: I hit 80% on my own 30-question benchmark, but only 56% on BIRD Mini-Dev’s 50 public questions. Nine experiments later, I had ruled out the multi-candidate hypothesis from three different angles. What’s left is schema understanding and methodology.

item.title: 5. AEO - Why Coding Agents Read Documentation Differently
item.description: If GEO optimizes for consumer AI, AEO optimizes for coding agents. This article covers document length constraints, llms.txt, skill.md, and AGENTS.md — the files that matter.

item.title: 8. 30-Question Retail Sample at 80%: Four PDCA Cycles
item.description: After wiring up the router, I ran a 30-question benchmark and pushed NL2SQL EX (Execution Accuracy) from 66.67% to 80%. Here’s what I fixed across four cycles and where things broke.

item.title: 7. When a Question Comes In, Who Decides the Routing?
item.description: The term definitions are done. But when a user asks a question, who decides whether to search the graph, write SQL, or run a vector search? Things I ran into while designing the router.

item.title: 6. When You Don't Have to Build Agent Infra Yourself, Harnesses Become Obsolete. What About the Ontology?
item.description: Shortly after the Conway leak, Anthropic officially launched Claude Managed Agents. As agent infrastructure gets absorbed into platforms, here’s why DataNexus’s ontology layer remains safe.

item.title: 5. Automating Metadata Maintenance: Karpathy's LLM Wiki Architecture
item.description: RAG starts from scratch every time. Karpathy proposes having the LLM maintain a wiki directly so knowledge accumulates. DataNexus’s ontology catalog needs the same principle to avoid abandonment.

item.title: 4. Off-Site GEO - How to Win Over AI That Ignores Your Official Site
item.description: Even with perfect On-Site GEO, half of AI citations come from external channels. We cover platform-specific Off-Site strategies and how to diagnose your robots.txt setup.

item.title: 3. On-Site GEO Technical Architecture - From Product DB to JSON-LD
item.description: How product master DB data flows through a 3-stage pipeline to become JSON-LD in your HTML <head>. Covers the pipeline architecture and SSR-based automated deployment.

item.title: 2. Each AI Cites Different Sources
item.description: ChatGPT favors Wikipedia, Perplexity leans on Reddit, and Gemini prefers official websites. Covering all AI platforms with a single strategy is impossible.

item.title: 1. What Is GEO - AI Citation Strategy Beyond SEO
item.description: Only 9% of Google’s top 10 pages are cited by AI. In an era where SEO rankings no longer guarantee AI citations, we break down the three core principles of GEO and their academic foundations.

item.title: 4. Super-Sub Types — Can a Customer Be Both Individual and Corporate?
item.description: Super-sub types clarify business classifications at the logical model level. When converting to a physical model, three options emerge — and in a DW, that choice reshapes the entire dimension design.

item.title: 3. ERD Notation — Same Diagram, Different Interpretation
item.description: Same Crow’s Foot, different meaning. A single dashed line means different things in different tools. If you want models to serve as a shared language on your project, start by aligning on notation.

item.title: 2. OLTP vs DW Models — Different Purpose, Different Design
item.description: Even when the ERDs look similar, the design philosophies are completely different. OLTP is about transactional integrity; DW is about analytical access paths. That difference creates unfamiliar things like Unknown records and point-in-time data.

item.title: 4. Why We Added a SKOS Compatibility Layer
item.description: Why we chose SKOS to connect the DataNexus ontology with external systems. Designing a compatibility layer between LPG and RDF – two different graph models.

item.title: 1. Is Kimball Still Relevant in the Cloud DW Era?
item.description: How DW modeling considerations have shifted with Synapse, BigQuery, and Redshift. Kimball, Data Vault, One Big Table — practical criteria for choosing the right approach.

item.title: 3. Can DataHub's Glossary Work as an Ontology?
item.description: We tried using DataHub’s Business Glossary as an ontology store. What worked, what didn’t, and how we worked around it.

item.title: 2. How We Chose These 4 Open-Source Tools
item.description: How we decided on DataHub + Vanna + ApeRAG + DozerDB for DataNexus. What got eliminated from the candidate list, and why.

item.title: 1. Why We're Building DataNexus
item.description: The structural problems with enterprise data analytics, and why we decided to build an ontology-based data agent platform.

item.title: About
item.description: Junho Lee - Data & AI Platform Architect

item.title: Development Roadmap
item.description: DataNexus enterprise ontology-based NL2SQL autonomous data agent platform development roadmap and real-time progress
